summary:
The Windover Archaeological Site in Brevard County, Florida, USA, is a pond burial site where Early Archaic hunter-gatherer communities interred their dead in a small lake approximately 7000 to 8000 years ago. Discovered during construction of a housing development in 1982 and subsequently excavated by Florida State University archaeologists, the site yielded the remains of at least 168 individuals preserved by the waterlogged, anaerobic conditions of the pond — including brain tissue preserved in several skulls, providing the oldest known collection of human brain tissue in the Americas.

The Windover burials are remarkable for several reasons. The well-preserved brains allowed ancient DNA analysis, contributing important genetic data about early Florida populations. The preservation conditions also maintained organic materials including woven fabric — representing some of the oldest textiles found in the Americas, woven from plant fibers using sophisticated techniques that indicate advanced textile knowledge well before the adoption of agriculture in this region. The textiles demonstrate that these Early Archaic communities possessed considerable craft sophistication despite a hunter-gatherer economy.

The Windover community appears to have returned repeatedly to the same pond over generations, suggesting a semi-permanent attachment to this location within their seasonal round. The careful treatment of the dead — who were buried wrapped in fabric and weighted with stakes to prevent the bodies from floating — reflects complex mortuary practices and social concern for the deceased. The site provides a window into a North American Archaic way of life that was simultaneously materially sophisticated and fully adapted to the rich subtropical resources of the Florida peninsula.
